data.title: Tesla Q1 Results: Sales Stagnate, Risky Ventures Burn Through Cash
data.description:
Elon Musk-led Tesla, Inc.'s (TSLA) release of its first quarter (Q1) earnings for its Fiscal Year (FY) 2026 after market close on the 22nd of April didn't immediately reverse the trend in the stock, which was down 14% relative to the start of the year as of the day before the release. The stock was essentially flat on the day of the release and went on to drop 3.6%.

However, there is potentially some qualified solace for long-time Tesla bulls: early trends in FY 2026 - strictly in balance sheet items - indicate that it will (at best) mirror trends in the previous two FYs, without any immediately apparent signs of worsening.

Trend Drilldown

Early trends indicate that overall revenue is poised to be weakly negative to flat relative to the past two FYs:

While the total cost of revenue is inching towards showing signs of improved efficiency - particularly in automotive sales, leasing, and energy - spends on R&D takes overall operating expenses towards at least a 20% growth over the previous FY - in line with general trends since 2023.

If early trends in the bottom line (i.e., earnings or net income) continue, net income shrinking will largely mirror the past two FYs, with a 48% reduction relative to the previous FY.

Interestingly, in terms of line item percentage share of total revenue, the company is arguably a picture of stability relative to FY 2025.

Like the previous FY, automotive sales and leasing account for a little over 70% of all revenue, while the likes of the Supercharging network, service operations on the massive quantities of vehicles sold over past years, and so forth continue to progressively inch up in meaningfulness towards revenue contribution.

In the overall production and delivery mix, little has changed since FY 2025. Model 3/Y continues to account for 97% of all vehicles produced, and overall production is almost exactly a fourth of volumes produced across FY 2025. Other models, however, spiked to 5% share of deliveries in the quarter and might possibly smooth out into the 3-5% share seen since 2021.

This "lack of worsening" did appear as a surprise relative to analysts' consensus estimates. While it ordinarily would have registered as a positive signal for the stock, the market likely digested the news in context; alongside an overall chill in new vehicle sales evident across the U.S. (Tesla's main market) and the massive premium the stock enjoys relative to other carmakers, there was little impetus to or appetite for the stock.

That is, if the company were to be considered a "carmaker" - a notion that Elon Musk has been hard at work in an attempt to dispel over the past several quarters.

New Directions While Digging In

A positive development in the quarter was a strong $1.44 billion in free cash flow. However, CFO Vaibhav Taneja cautioned that free cash flow will be negative for the rest of the year as the company spends on its ongoing endeavors that support Musk's bid to reframe the Tesla story: its ongoing work in AI, robotics, autonomous driving and - now - chip fabrication.

The earnings release highlighted that the Cortex 2 AI Training Compute cluster - needed for both autonomous driving and its humanoid robot project - is now online and has started training workloads. The first-generation production line for its Optimus humanoid robots - with a designed capacity of 1 million robots a year—will be replacing the Model S and Model X lines in Fremont and is scheduled to begin in Q2 2026. The second-generation line under development in Gigafactory Texas is designed for a long-term annual production capacity of 10 million robots.

In April, the company completed the final chip design of its next-generation AI5 inference processor and, in partnership with SpaceX (also led by Musk), is preparing to build the world's largest chip fabrication facility - one that will take the vigorous vertical integration paradigm Tesla has taken in vehicle manufacturing into the chip space by integrating logic, memory, and packaging.

In the vehicle space, the company also announced that work continues in its new battery and material factories, which span from LFP cells in Nevada to cathode material and lithium refining in Texas. However, the company also states candidly that battery pack capacity continues to be a limitation on vehicle production ramp-ups.

In line with the upcoming launch of the Tesla Semi - the all-electric Class 8 semi-trailer truck designed for freight transport - the company also announced that it will be deploying public Megachargers, ultra-fast, high-power charging stations specifically designed for the Tesla Semi.

In the Q4 2025 report, the company has indicated a prospective map for Megacharger locations in 2026.

California and Texas are projected to have the highest density of these facilities as of now.

In Conclusion

The overall picture is decidedly a mixed bag. The large-scale commitment to manufacturing humanoid robots designed to supplant or augment human workforces is a bold and risky bet, especially when the question remains if the combined cost of AI compute and the robot fleet truly provides a cost differentiator against human labor.

Also in question - and in roughly similar terms - is the repeated commitment to building a fully autonomous Cybercab arm. While operating a "robotic Uber" is attractive on paper, regulatory approvals for large-scale deployment are still far away and a sizeable roadblock to surmount.

On the other hand, owning and operating a chip fabrication facility alongside a design lab is a definite long-term positive that opens up multiple addressable markets regardless of whether AI for robots or autonomous driving works out or not.

Another positive in a similar vein is the buildout of battery manufacturing for both vehicles and energy grid solutions. Electric Vehicles (EVs) are here to stay, and owning strong capabilities opens up interesting avenues in monetization in the future.

While the production of the Semi has been long-awaited, the relative paucity of charging infrastructure across the length and breadth of the land is poised to limit adoption in much the same way it did for passenger vehicles. While corporate fleet solutions are a tidy supplier demand, there is still a cap on competing with or supplanting the diesel-powered armadas of trucks rolling through the world's highways. A long ramp can be expected before the Semi becomes an attractive proposition worthy of consideration for investors.

However, in present-day terms, the fact remains that early trends in production volume stability indicate that the company is at an impasse in passenger EVs while other brands are registering some growth despite an outlook indicating a potential chilling effect looming on vehicle sales in the U.S. All in all, it's a company promising potential like it did during the 2010s while the market expects a steady translation of market share into value, and the company sinks cash into new, bold, and potentially risky endeavours.

All in all, a hold is in order.

data.title: Norfolk Southern: Watch It, But Union Pacific Is The Better Trade In The Deal Now (Earnings Review)
data.description:
Introduction

Norfolk Southern (NSC) just released its Q1 2026 earnings. Yet, the most important report for the stock was not its own but Union Pacific's (UNP). As the latter jumped up 7%, so did NSC. Since July 29, 2025, the two stocks have been more and more correlated. This makes sense because under the UNP-NSC deal, the math implies that NSC shareholders will receive, upon closing, 1 UNP share and $88.82 cash, which means that the implied value for NSC was $326 per share. The market is repricing the deal closure probability, and the more this happens, the more NSC trades up and down with UNP because their dollar moves converge.

However, in percentage terms, NSC should move a bit less than UNP because part of the deal is cash, and cash doesn't move with UNP's stock price. Once the deal was announced, there was room for an arbitrage play, where NSC shares were basically a call option on the deal closure. Today, the optionality on the deal is not cheap, and buying NSC today is more similar to a short put with a $4-$5 premium to absorb a risk of $88 per share if the deal breaks. This is not the payoff structure that people signed up for when they became NSC shareholders. If the deal closes, however, there is no real risk. Personally, I think the refiling the STB requested is actually a favorable event for the birth of the first American transcontinental Class 1 railroad. So, I don't think NSC shareholders should worry that much.

So, while I don't think NSC is a buy right now because of the pending deal, I still think that it is important to keep monitoring how the railroad is doing, both because it could soon be a key component of a new railroad and because it can tell us updated numbers on important economic activity in the country.

Norfolk Southern's Q1 2026 Earnings Review

When we look at NSC's results, we see that revenues were flat YoY to $3B. Merchandise reported volume growth of 1%, Intermodal was down by 4%, while coal, unsurprisingly, jumped up by 9% YoY. The total is that volume reported a 1% reduction, even though the revenue per unit increased by 1%. This means that pricing held.

When we look at the financial figures, we see something that is not exactly positive. Operating expense increased by 1%, the operating ratio (adjusted for the Eastern Ohio incident) deteriorated by 80 bps, and moved up to 68.7%. Norfolk Southern's operating income decreased by 2% to $939M, the same percentage decline as the company's net income, which was $597M. The diluted EPS were down by $0.04 YoY to $2.65, a 1.5% compression.

The CEO's remarks pointed out that severe weather was an issue. But I tend not to overweight this factor: weather will always be an issue with its unpredictability and volatility. However, it is true that Q1 is usually the weakest quarter. This means that operating expenses inflate if severe weather impacts rail traffic.

Let's take a moment to look at the commodity breakdown. We see that, beyond coal, the second best-performing commodity was chemicals. This is interesting because chemicals is usually used as a leading indicator of manufacturing activity. Since we have seen the manufacturing index move up in recent months, NSC offers us further confirmation of a trend that could be important for 2026.

The outlook that NSC's earnings imply is that the company could reach FY26 EPS around $11.50, which is $0.50 lower than the consensus. This is because I think that there will be three drags that need to be considered: merger expenses that are loading, customers choosing to dual-source while waiting for regulatory approval, and intermodal softness. Using an 18.5x earnings multiple, we have an implied share price of $213.

Last year, NSC ended up with $2.2B in FCF. This year, NSC announced that it will reduce its CapEx to $1.9B, which makes sense: if the deal goes through, CapEx will then have to be deployed for the integration. This is something that makes sense. After all, a merger is extremely difficult among railroads, which are companies that own and operate infrastructure. To rationalize the network, harmonize the operating systems, and upgrade the terminals across the railroad, CapEx will be needed, probably above normal levels. Therefore, budgeting only $1.9B in capex for this year is a way to create extra flexibility by preserving FCF in anticipation of regulatory approval.

So, NSC on a standalone basis is worth around $213. If the deal breaks up, there is a reverse termination fee that could be worth around $11 per share. This means that the floor in case the deal breaks is $224. This is 30% below the current price, implying a downside close to $100, while only leaving a 1.4% arbitrage spread. This means that buying NSC today is similar to collecting a $4 premium to absorb a loss of $100. This is a reward-to-risk ratio of 1:20, which makes no sense.

I was a shareholder of NSC, as I bought it some time after the share price plummeted due to the consequences of the Eastern Ohio incident. However, I didn't have the time to build a full position. Therefore, I sold it a few months ago as I was taking some gains from smaller positions for two reasons. I was consolidating and concentrating my portfolio, but I was also raising cash as I am about to buy a new home.

Right now, it doesn't make sense to buy NSC. Investors should just wait for the STB to clear the horizon and then make a new decision as to what to do.

If one believes the deal won't go through, UNP is a much better option. If one believes that the deal will be approved (I am on this side of the camp), UNP is still the better option because it has less risk than NSC right now. Basically, if we use game theory, UNP is the dominant strategy for new capital regardless of what scenario materializes.

data.title: Buy 14 Ideal Graham Value All-Stars Of 27 'Safer' Dividends In 71 April/May GVAS
data.description:
Foreword

  • This article is one of 15 Dividend Dog collections features in Seeking Alpha by this reporter who tags himself, the "Dividend Dogcatcher."
  • I am Fredrik Arnold and, as the Dividend Dogcatcher, I have devised a series of twenty keystone essays that change little but for the essential graphic and statistical daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or annual data that support a cadre of over 30,000 followers.
  • Those fifteen keystone Dogcatcher essays include the following indices and publications:
  • Dogs of the Dow
  • S&P500
  • S&P600
  • Berkshire Hathaway
  • Fortune 500
  • Kiplinger Reports
  • Aristocrats
  • Dividend Kings
  • Barron's Pro Picks
  • Reader Favorites & /Rogues (ReFa/Ro)
  • Sustainables (Barron's)
  • Bloomberg Intelligence
  • Power Dividends
  • Ben Graham Value All Stars (GVAS)
  • Monthly Payers (MoPays)

To learn the 14 Ideal Graham All-Star equities paying "safer" dividends, see the conclusion of this article, in the Afterword. (You might even start at the afterword and work your way back to the front.)

About Large Cap Value

"A Value ranking for large cap stocks from YCharts puts together complementary strategies found during stock research. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most value, and 1's are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc.).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will infrequently lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios." -YCharts

While over half of this collection of GASV is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny or no forward looking dividends, fourteen of the twenty-seven "safer" lowest-priced dogs of the GASV are buyable.

April/May data found: BW LPG (BWLP), IRSA Inversiones (IRS); Weibo Corp (WB); Altria Group; Global Ship (GSL); Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentinaria (BBVA); Verizon (VZ); Main Street Capital (MAIN); Equinor ASA (EQNR); AT&T (T); Orix (IX); Deutsche Bank (DB); NuSkin Enterprises (NUS); Costamare (CMRE), on the buy-list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced April/May GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all fourteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 24.48% To 109.05% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By April/May, 2027

Seven of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April/May, 2026 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 70% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target-prices, as reported by YCharts, defined the 2026-27 projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected for April/May, 2027 were:

Immersion (IMMR) was projected to net $1,090.46 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% under the market as a whole.

Weibo was projected to net $464.64 based on the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 34% over the market as a whole.

IRSA Inversiones was projected to net $436.82 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 55% over the market as a whole.

Noah Holdings (NOAH) was projected to net $406.07, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 20% under the market as a whole.

Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) was projected to net $319.81, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 70% under the market as a whole.

Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) was projected to net $287.11, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% under the market as a whole.

The RMR Group (RMR) was projected to net $256.12 based on the median of target prices estimated by 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% under the market as a whole.

Ennis (EBS) was projected to net $255.18, based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 68% less than the market as a whole.

NatWest Group (NWG) was projected to net $236.67 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% under the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer (ET) was projected to net $234.84, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 38% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 39.88% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten GASV stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 0.16% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top 43 April/May, 2025 GASV By 1yr Total Returns

Top 50 April/May, 2025 GASV By Analyst Target Gains

Top 50 April/May, 2025 GASV By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top April/May GASV Stocks By Yield Ranged 7.09% To 12.56% Per YCharts

Top ten GASV came from three of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Four financial services stalwarts placed first, third, fifth, and ninth, Blue Owl Capital [1], Noah Holdings [3], Ares Capital (ARCC) [5], and NatWest Group [9].

Thereafter, four energy members entered the top ten, placing second, sixth, eighth, and tenth: BW LPG Ltd [2]; Alliance Resource Partners [6]; MPLX LP (MPLX) [8]; Energy Transfer [10].

Finally. two real estate representatives placed fourth and seventh, The RMR Group [4], and IRSA Inversiones [7], to complete the April/May Dogs of the GASV by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten Mid-March GASV by dividend and price as of market close 4/20/2026.

This month none of the top-ten GASV by yield showed less than ideal status. The prices of all ten are below the projected annual dividends from $1k invested. Yes, all ten are IDEAL. But not all are "safer." Several are paying dividends with borrowed money.

Costs per $1 of Dividend

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. That condition was reached by forty of the seventy-one highest yield April/May GASV.

Sixty-six forward looking dividend reporting stocks out of the seventy-one April/May GASV Costs Per $1 of dividend ranged from $7.96 for Blue Owl Capital, to $509.42 for Progressive Corp (PGR).

Actionable Conclusion (21): Eighteen of Seventy-One Dividend Paying GASV Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash. Eighteen GASV so infected are considered unsafe to buy. They are paying dividends from borrowed money

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own it. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead: dread the Fed, the toll of trading terms, wars, tacos, and uncertainty.

No Price Drops or Dividend Increases Are Needed for Ten April/May Top-Yield GASV "Fair-Priced" For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all in the top ten are already ideally priced. The results from that top chart show that all ten are more than 15% away from having a share price greater than divided paid from $1k invested..

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 16.08% To 105.48% Top Ten GASV Upsides To April/May 2027; (32) Four Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 21.30% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of April 20, 2026

Ten-top GASVs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten GASV selected 4/20/26 by yield, represented three of eleven sectors. Communications, Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Health Care, Industrials Materials , Technology, and Utilities, all missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend GASV (34) To Deliver 32.26% Vs. (35) 26.59% Net Gains by All Ten Come April/May, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten GASV by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 21.30% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The third lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, IRSA Inversiones, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 43.68%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield GASV dogs for April/May were Noah Holdings, Blue Owl Capital, Noah IRSA Inversiones, NatWest Group, and The RMR Group, with prices ranging from $10.28 to $17.06.

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of March 10 were: BW LPG, Energy Transfer, Ares Capital, Alliance Resource, and MPLX LP, with prices ranging from $18.54 to $55.69.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the new year and March madness dies and the feckless Tech-fueled market inflation and dithering presidential deals gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of the few memes, look for more GASV to become fair-priced… slow and sure, or very suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 14 Ideal of 27 'Safer'April/May GASV Dogs

April/May 2026, 27 of the 71 GASV were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 4/20/26 data showed 14 of those were fair-priced and IDEAL to buy at this time.

How GASV 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced "Fair" Price Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that dividends from $1K invested in eight of our top ten 'safer' dividend GASV conform to the dogcatcher ideal of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those eight ideal dogs were: BW LPG Ltd, IRSA, Weibo, Altria, Global Ship, Banco Bilbao, Verizon, and Main Street Capital.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called, "fair price."

Eight of ten share prices of these "safer" survivors are below the dividend payouts from $1k invested. The top chart reveals two prices that would need to drop 6.58% to 27.07% so as not to exceed the dividend payout from $1k invested.

These GASV leaders fetch dividends about as "good" as they can get.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher GASV updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" and "Ideal" GASV dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com

data.title: Ares Capital's Drop Offers The Opportunity To Capture A 10%+ Yield
data.description:
Over the last few months now, things have been really rough for shareholders of Ares Capital (ARCC). Even with the yield factored in, the total return for the BDC has been negative to the tune of 8.9% since I reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate in late January of this year. From that time through today, meanwhile, the S&P 500 has inched higher by 0.8%. This returned disparity is disappointing to say the least. However, in my view, the market is underestimating the potential of the business. It's now currently trading at a discount to NAV, and it is yielding a hefty 10.1% as of this writing. Near term, it is entirely possible that we could see downward pressure. But I believe that, over time, it will turn out to be an attractive prospect that will generate market returns or something even better, especially if my fears about the economy deteriorating proved to be correct. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate makes sense here.

Before the market opens on April 28th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of this entity’s 2026 fiscal year variant the good news as we approach that date is that analysts are currently optimistic that revenue and profitability will rise year over year. Naturally, the picture can change and we should always be on the lookout given the nature of this business and how it could change based on the interest rate environment.

Checking in on Ares Capital

At its core, Ares Capital operates as the largest publicly traded BDC out there. Its focus is on raising capital on the public markets on the cheap, investing that capital into private enterprises that need it, and capturing that spread between the amount that it pays for capital and the amount that it collects from its investments, all in order to generate a positive return for investors. Conceptually, I find this to be an interesting space. And one of the great things about this enterprise is that it actually has a rather liberal definition of middle market. Some of the BDCs that invest in middle market firms define those as enterprises with EBITDA of between $50 million and $150 million. However, Ares Capital what's the range between $10 million and $250 million. While that does increase the risk profile of it on the bottom end of the spectrum, it also opens up the door to greater returns if things go right.

This is a rather attractive investment opportunity. Management pointed out in their latest investor presentation that sources of capital for private firms have dried up to an extent over the last few decades. For instance, there were somewhere around 14,000 banks in the US back in 1980. But by the third quarter of 2025, that number was somewhere around 3,000. And yet, the prospect for funding here is massive. Management believes that companies in the US that generate between $100 million and $1 billion a year in revenue offer an opportunity of around $3 trillion. But when you add on top of that larger firms with revenues in excess of $1 billion that it could target, that number climbs to $5.4 trillion.

Over the last 12 month window, the company had and origination funnel of almost $1 trillion of transactions that it reviewed. However, the enterprise is incredibly picky about where it puts its money. For instance, it has closed at least one investment with around 515 different financial sponsors. And, in the most recent quarter, it had investments in 603 portfolio companies. About 24% of its portfolio is focused on the software and services space. 11% involves health care equipment and services, while 9% is associated with commercial and professional services.

Even though the enterprise has a large number of holdings in its portfolio, it has made sure to remain quite diverse. Only about 0.2% of its portfolio is tied up in its average holding, with the largest of these accounting for just 1.3% of its portfolio. Its top 10 investments accounted for just 10.8% of assets at the end of 2025. That compares to 24.1% for the average peer firm in this industry. All of this means that, even if things do get tough, the firm should be quite resilient against losses. Of course, that doesn't mean that it is completely immune. Even weakening economic conditions could push companies across multiple industries lower.

One thing that management does to reduce risk further is to be deliberate about exactly what it invests investor capital into. 61% of its investments are allocated toward first lien senior secured loans. Another 5% is tied up in second lien senior secured loans while senior debt accounts for about 9% of its asset base. Preferred equity accounts for just 9%, while other equity is responsible for 8%. So even if times do get challenging, the nature of its investments should protect it.

One of my big complaints about these high yielding opportunities has often been that they pay out more to shareholders than they bring in. This means that either distributions must eventually be cut or investors will see a deterioration in NAV. This is not the case when it comes to Ares Capital. In 2025, for instance, the company saw profits plus realized gains and losses amount to just under $1.40 billion. It paid out $1.26 billion in the form of distributions. This meant a surplus amounting to $131 million. Now, admittedly, it did see $174 million in unrealized losses during that time. But that was a rather irregular window of time for the business. Over the last four years, the cumulative impact of unrealized gains and losses has been negative for the business to the tune of $161 million. That compares to cumulative profits and realized gains amounting to $5 billion. When you do the math by taking out cumulative distributions of $4.35 billion, you end up with an excess over the last four years of $496 million.

Management has been successful in actually growing NAV per share over the last few years. Back in 2021, NAV was $18.96. Although that did drop to $18.40 and 2022, it has been climbing ever since. By 2025, it increased to $19.94. As of this writing, its current unit price is $18.63, meaning that it is trading at a slight discount to NAV. This is great in and of itself because it offers some upside for investors. And it helps to explain why the yield has risen to 10.1%.

As the chart below illustrates, the total return Ares Capital over the last five years was actually higher than the market more broadly up until recently. Starting around the middle of last year, we started to see some underperformance even as the market moved up. But even with this, the 54.23% total return captured over the last five years is not terribly different from the 50.62% that the S&P 500 of equal weighted index achieved, or the 55.88% that the Dow Jones Industrial Average captured. It is, unfortunately, below the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ. But I would argue that, with the yield that it currently offers, the potential for market beating returns should times get tough like I suspect they will, is certainly there.

[object HTMLElement]

This doesn't mean that the company won't face some difficult adjustments. The fact of the matter is that it is significantly exposed to what happens with interest rates. As interest rates drop, it becomes increasingly likely that those firms that borrowed from it will pay off their debt earlier by refinancing with other companies or will enter into negotiations to refinance with Ares Capital. It also doesn't help that, as of the end of 2025, 72% of its investments at fair value had interest rates or dividends that were at variable rates as opposed to fixed. Only 12% bore fixed rates. 9% were tied to non-income producing activities, 1% involved non-accrual status invest, and 6% involved certain equity.

In the table above, you can see what the impact would be to interest and dividend income, interest expense, and net profits, for the company in different interest rate climates. Overall net profits windfall by $114 million if interest rates drop by 100 basis points. And if they fall by 300 basis points, the decline would be $296 million. Considering that the firm generated net profits of $1.30 billion last year, this would not be an insignificant decline in profitability for it and could trigger a reduction in how much it is paying out to investors. But even if that did occur and if the firm had to cut its distribution by that decline in profitability, we would still be looking at a yield on a forward basis of about 9.18% assuming that the share price remained unchanged. Considering how the market is right now, I still find that incredibly compelling.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year before the market opens on April 28th. The current expectation is that revenue, defined as total investment income, is expected to climb from $732 million last year to $776 million this year. And with that should come a rise in profits on a per share basis from $0.36 to $0.48. That would mean an increase in overall profitability from $241 million to $336 million.

Naturally, I would encourage investors to keep an eye on those data points. But there are some other things that should be looked at as well. For instance, I have already discussed how impacted by changes in interest rates the enterprise could be. Given broader economic conditions, I think at some point the Federal Reserve will begin cutting rates. So I would love to see any update management has regarding sensitivity there and what their expectations are moving forward. I would also be encouraged by their assessment of the health of their portfolio companies and the economy. If I am correct about the state of the economy, we will start to see some deterioration at some point. So what management has to say when that day comes could be very important.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the market is getting this one wrong. It's not that I don't expect some weakness in financial performance as economic conditions deteriorate. Rather, I expect that the weakness in question will be more favorable than the pain that we might see from the decline in the market. In the meantime, investors get to capture a double digit yield, and that alone, when you add on top of it the fact that management is not being reckless with distributions, justifies a ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: AB Volvo: NA Truck Lines Are Just About To Scale
data.description:
AB Volvo (VOLAF)(VLVLY) put in a relatively resilient performance in organic terms, ignoring the FX effects from having a significant North American presence and reporting in SEK. While there was some moderation of demand in their end markets, not least because of tariffs where there is plenty of domestic US competition, they are ramping some of their new offerings which we discussed in our previous coverage with production stops now overcome, benefiting from learning economies, while also divesting and discontinuing some less profitable business lines. In terms of mix, there was also a pickup in service as part of revenue, which should be 50%+ higher in margin than the vehicles sales revenue.

Results

On a headline, unadjusted basis, services shrunk slower than vehicles. On an organic basis service revenues grew 6% organically against flat organic growth for the vehicles business. These positive mix effects accrued over both the heavy construction and trucks business apparently, and lead to an overall expansion in organically adjusted EBIT margin, helped also by some discontinued businesses, such as Rokbak which lost too much scale also due to the Russian market falling out, as well as some other divestments that made the business more lean. Adjusted operating income was down less than 10% thanks to the positive mix effects from discontinuations and general lineup revamp, with the CE mix effects seen in the resilient operating income performance for the segment. Negative pressures on the adjusted income were freight costs, which will climb into the coming quarter on account of the Iran War and higher oil prices likely dominating more of Q2 than they did of Q1, as well as tariff costs which would have pressured margins. There was also a positive effect from net R&D on account of the major developments, concentrated it would seem on Mack Trucks, now in the rear view with the commercialisation of these new offerings the current challenge, perhaps dragging for the last time on the current results with blue skies ahead. R&D was down almost 15% in expenses.

The outlook for trucks is quite decent, excepting of course macro pressures that might result from a possibly ongoing Iran War (Germany for example cut growth outlook), and the construction equipment market is also beginning to look up a bit with book to bills above 1x and YoY growth organically of 16%, adjusted for more than just FX. There has also been some positive moves in buses, driven by Prevost, concentrated on North America.

Similar to in our previous coverage, what we are most focused on is the pressures still ongoing on revamps in the Truck offering in the North American market.

For trucks, our European business performance compensated the under-absorption cost from the U.S. truck manufacturing operations that were standing still approximately 25%-30% of the available time in the quarter... we now have available production capacity to meet the increased demand we have seen recently. As from May, we are in balance in North America.

~Martin Lundstedt, CEO of Volvo Group, Q1 Earnings

These model changeovers were becoming a pressure on results as of the last call we covered as well, and Trucks, usually the stalwart business, was the underperformer for the moment. With considerable underabsorption this quarter on a quarter dominated by total production stops, we expect considerable operating income improvement coming from truck vehicle contribution as the production ramps, with associated fixed costs in R&D having also come down.

Bottom Line

Service pick up is already positive, and the adjusted profits have been defensible. This quarter does mark a transition, including discontinuations and new launches, that we expect to prime profitability as industrial scale kicks in. The SEK price of Volvo is 323, with 16 SEK in annualized EPS that's around a 20x estimated annualized P/E, based on these first quarter results where income is being penalized by effects that should mean current profits are a bit of a bottom for the company as Mack scales and other profit measures kick in. Despite these temporary pressures on profits, the PE is still lower than something like PACCAR Inc. (PCAR) at 28x. We continue to prefer Volvo relatively. However, this isn't a particularly pro-cyclical business. Higher capital costs could hit real estate again and cut at the legs a construction equipment recovery. It is a broad based risk due to the systemic importance of oil. Additionally, growth hits could crimp on logistics markets and affect book build for trucks, but a baseline level of scaling with production stops going into the rear view seems inevitable to us and we'd expect some amount of rebound in operating profit regardless.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Rare Chance To Buy Blackstone's $1.3 Trillion Empire At A Deep Discount
data.description:
I last covered Blackstone Inc. (BX) back in August 2025 and rated the stock a Hold. Since then, BX has pulled back sharply, in large part due to negative media narratives around the private credit space, in which BX has significant exposure. In this article, I am going to re-look at the firm in light of its recently released Q1 results and share why I think it is an attractive buy on its recent dip.

My previous Hold rating was based on its stretched valuation multiple, as well as the meaningful macro and geopolitical risks that BX is exposed to, given its global presence, including substantial exposure to China. However, the significant decline in its stock price, despite continued underlying growth, means that it has now moved into value territory in my view.

Q1 Earnings: The Numbers Tell a Different Story

Its Q1 results told that story. For example, distributable earnings came in up 25% year over year and beat consensus estimates in the process, at $1.36 per share. Fee-related earnings also came in 23% higher than the year-ago period. In fact, it was one of the three best fee-related earnings quarters in the company's history, its best non-Q4 result ever. Total management fees came in at a record $2.1 billion and were up 13% year over year. Base management fee growth was up 14% in private equity, 15% in credit, and 21% in Blackstone Multi-Asset Investing (BXMA).

Assets under management were 12% higher than a year ago and came in at a whopping $1.3 trillion, making it the largest alternative asset manager in the world. Over the past 12 months alone, it has raised $250 billion, and that pace remained very strong in Q1 at $69 billion. The company also has substantial net accrued performance revenue of $7 billion, which was up 9% year over year and at its highest level in three and a half years, reflecting $5.69 per share of embedded future earnings power.

Infrastructure and AI: BX's Most Powerful Growth Engine

The company's growth is being driven primarily by its infrastructure business, which grew by a whopping 41% year over year to $84 billion. Its infrastructure business has delivered a 19% net annual return since inception, well above its original 10 to 12% target rate. In Q1 alone, its infrastructure funds appreciated 7.8%, reflecting 25% 12-month appreciation.

Its data center business is seeing particularly strong growth, with a $160 billion data center pipeline making it arguably the world's largest private investor in AI-related infrastructure. As a result, BX is even looking to launch a new public company for its data center business, which should help create a very attractive source of permanent capital for the firm. BX also owns the longest cross-country natural gas pipeline network in the United States, which should profit immensely from natural gas demand fueled by the data center boom in the United States.

Credit Platform: Institutions Are Voting With Their Capital

The company's credit platform, despite being subject to a barrage of negative media headlines, continued to grow at a 15% year-over-year clip, with $40 billion worth of inflows in Q1 alone. In fact, it was one of the best quarters ever for fundraising from institutions and insurance clients. That said, much of the strong growth was seen in areas outside of direct lending, with its investment-grade private credit platform growing 23% year over year. Its insurance business grew by 18% year over year.

The company's direct lending private fund, known as Blackstone Private Credit Fund (BCRED), on the other hand, saw an outflow of $1.4 billion in the first quarter due to retail investors being influenced by negative media press. Institutional demand for the fund remained strong, and most importantly, BCRED's performance remains strong at a 9.4% net annualized rate of return since inception, which is about 60% above the leveraged loan index over the same period of time. BX's opportunistic credit fund also posted strong growth, with over $10 billion of fundraising, and was meaningfully oversubscribed during the quarter. It is also worth noting that BX insiders helped to meet redemption requests at BCRED that were over the 5% gate, thereby effectively doubling down even more than many of BX's peers on private credit, reflecting significant confidence. BX also continues to remain very bullish on the private wealth channel, as assets under management in this segment grew by 14% year over year, including in its private equity, REIT, and additional product pipelines. This brought total private wealth sales to $10 billion in Q1, including $7 billion for perpetual SaaS strategies.

Why the Risk-Reward Has Flipped in BX's Favor

Overall, BX's portfolio continues to generate strong performance across all of its strategies in terms of total returns for investors, and fundraising remains strong everywhere, with the primary sticking point being in its retail-focused BCRED fund. When you combine the overall strength for BX with its attractive valuation due to its significant pullback, attractive dividend yield, and continued strong growth momentum, I think it looks like an attractive buy on the dip.

Of course, no investment is risk-free. Continued macro uncertainty, both in the economic and geopolitical domains, could threaten to weaken deal flow, performance, and some of its more internationally exposed funds, as well as its fundraising. Continued negative press around private credit could weigh on one of its stronger growth engines, even though institutions and other credit-related strategies are driving strong growth in its overall credit business right now. It is this weakness in its retail direct lending business, however, that likely drove the stock lower in the immediate aftermath of it reporting its Q1 results.

It's also worth noting that BX, at $1.3 trillion in assets under management, needs to raise a lot of money to continue driving growth. It is also worth noting that BX does not have all permanent capital, so there is a need to constantly raise capital simply to maintain its current AUM level.

Why I'm Upgrading BX to Strong Buy, and What's Still Holding It Back

While BX is not my highest conviction pick in the alternative asset management space due to the fact that it is a bit more geopolitically exposed than some and is also unlikely to grow as rapidly as some of its smaller peers due to its immense size, and of course its valuation is not quite as compelling as some of its peers, I still think that BX is a very attractive buy on the dip. I'm upgrading it to a strong buy accordingly. As a result, it is on our watch list as we continue to favor other alternative asset managers and run a 7–8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market beta portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: KB Financial: Staying Bullish On Earnings Beat And ROE Expansion Potential
data.description:
I've kept KB Financial Group Inc. (KB) (105560.KS) as a Buy-rated name.

The stock is attractively-valued based on the comparison of its "Return On Equity/ROE" and P/B multiple. The 1Q2026 results were also a favorable surprise.

The firm's generous stockholder returns and street-beating 1Q25 numbers were highlighted in my earlier April 30, 2025 write-up.

1Q2026 Earnings Surpassed Expectations

The group issued a 6-K disclosing its latest financials this Thursday.

KB's Jan-Mar '26 bottom-line went up 12% to ₩1.89T on a year-on-year basis. As per S&P Capital IQ, that was 8% above the analysts' ₩1,753B forecast.

In my opinion, outperformance in non-banking businesses, leaner operations, and stable asset quality provided major tailwinds.

Its non-interest income was 28% higher YoY at ₩1,651B for 1Q26. Also, the non-bank entities' bottom-line contribution grew 1ppt to 43% over the same period. Both figures represented new historic three-month peaks.

I think that the brokerage & wealth management arm "KB Securities" was a stand-out. This unit's "Assets Under Management" or "AUM" grew 56% YoY to ₩240T in the recent quarter.

Separately, KB reported a 1Q2026 "Cost-to-Income Ratio/CIR" of 35.4%. That's 400bps below what it achieved for full-year 2025. Management attributed this to "ongoing efforts to enhance workforce efficiency" at the results meeting.

Its first quarter net profit was also boosted by a 25% YoY reduction in credit impairment charges to ₩493B.

KB's "Non-Performing Assets/NPA" ratio remained comfortable at 0.73% for 1Q26. That's even better than 1Q25's 0.76%. My take is that its healthy loan book allowed for lower provisioning.

Anticipating Financial Return Improvement In FY2026

The company's ROE went from FY24's 9.7% to FY25's 10.9%. My bet is on another 50bps increase to 11.4% this year.

I've identified several levers which are outlined below.

A key driver is "Net Interest Margin" or "NIM" accretion in my view.

At the 4Q25 call in February, management guided for "a gradual decline of NIM for 2026." But KB revised its outlook to a "slight increase" on April 23.

The change in stance is consistent with third-party research organizations' expectations. ING's analysis suggests that "BoK (Bank of Korea) is likely to respond with rate hikes" assuming that "supply constraints" drive up inflation.

I also like how KB is actively lowering its "Cost of Funds/CoF". Its Q1 briefing emphasized "the expansion of core deposits and the rebalancing of the funding portfolio."

A further penetration of overseas markets is another thing you should watch closely.

The firm aims to raise the income share from non-Korean operations from a mid-single-digit percentage last year to high-single-digit levels in FY2026.

According to the Thursday meeting disclosures, its Indonesian affiliate Bukopin's "IT system was upgraded after restructuring for many years." I believe the conclusion of this systems overhaul puts it in a position to capitalize on local opportunities.

"Indonesian banking sector remained stable with a loan growth at 9.6% year-on-year in 2025" as per Bank Rakyat's (BKRKY) Q4 transcript. KB's KRW-denominated advances rose by a more modest 3.8% during the same timeframe. This sends me a clear message regarding the huge potential of emerging economy Bukopin operates in.

I'm also encouraged by the group's strong intent to reshape its portfolio.

Capital Deployment Framework

As per the chart presented above, the bank will be pivoting away from sub-segments that deliver sub-par "Return on Risk-Weighted Assets" or "RoRWA." The funds subsequently freed up will then be re-invested in high-RoRWA verticals. At the 1st quarter meeting, it also stressed that the future focus will be on "high-quality customers."

On the same day as its results release, KB also announced "the cancellation of (14.3M) existing treasury shares" by mid-May.

I estimate that the company's equity base will shrink by almost 4% when the above-mentioned transaction is done. This enhances its future returns by lowering the denominator of the ROE equation.

Shares Are Mispriced

KB is now trading at a trailing price-to-book of 1.04x as per S&P Capital IQ.

I'm valuing this name with the "Gordon Growth Model/GGM." I've assumed an 8% cost of equity (market average), a 3% perpetuity growth (mature economy proxy), and a 11.4% ROE (my prediction).

The target P/B calculated is 1.68 times, implying a +62% (1.68/1.04) upside.

Even if I choose to be conservative, the stock remains meaningfully undervalued. Changing the ROE input to FY25's actual 10.9% will still yield a more demanding P/NAV of 1.58x.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

Prolonged geopolitical instability could hurt the global economy. KB's banking and brokerage businesses are potential victims, being cyclical in nature.

The firm's prospects are also dependent on the success of self-help measures like portfolio restructuring and funding cost optimization. If management executes poorly on these related initiatives, its future ROE could disappoint investors.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm still positive on this stock.

KB's better-than-anticipated 1Q bottom-line is impressive. Its ROE uptrend is sustainable based on my assessment.

data.title: Marvell: Finally Silences The Skeptics, But Don't Rush In Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Marvell Fights Back Against The Skeptics

The AI boom is spreading wildly.

While software folks have been under the weather, the ones building the infrastructure have nothing much to complain about.

And this time, AI networking plays are now on fire!

We already know that memory and storage players continue to be significant beneficiaries, but the focal point has quickly shifted to the networking guys.

The result from this fervor has also seen the stock of Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) break the ceiling of its previous highs with such force that I’m still trying to catch my breath from the surge this week.

In just two months, MRVL has arguably eviscerated the negative sentiments that saw the stock failing to gain traction, as semiconductor stocks continue to print new peaks.

Of course, I’m also concerned with that hockey stick vertical spike in its print, because I don’t think the price action is sustainable without a meaningful retracement.

That said, the fundamental underpinnings for Marvell’s growth thesis through at least 2027 look to be highly secure, following a spate of deals and recent developments.

And for the second fiddle in the networking and the custom XPUs space, the “revenge” has never felt so sweet, especially when panned against Broadcom’s (AVGO) recent performance, outperforming its larger peer since late 2025.

Marvell Brings Nvidia On Board

So, has Marvell finally arrived on the grand stage with the recent victories? Let’s see.

Nvidia's (NVDA) $2B investment opens up a much deeper collaboration than ever before.

I don’t expect anything significant to come out of that in the short term, but the optics of it are good.

Bringing aboard Nvidia’s money, albeit a relatively small sum compared to investments in Intel (INTC), OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO), etc., consolidates Marvell's prowess when contending against Broadcom.

Sure, it’s not as if I think Nvidia and Marvell will be doing a lot of networking or custom chips together for now.

But, I think it allows Nvidia’s partners or customers to now have the chance to work closer with Marvell if they want to go through NVLink Fusion.

Back in my previous MRVL write-up, I had already anticipated that the market was simply too downcast on its prospects.

It just didn’t make a lot of sense to me that the market priced a high-growth company within the optical networking space below its 5-year average.

And, we also haven’t considered the XPU custom opportunity beyond Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT)?

Because that was exactly what happened, right?

Marvell Benefits From The Amazon-Anthropic Deal

I don’t think I need to repeat it, but just a timely reminder on the extension of the partnership between Amazon and Anthropic.

This upside could be massive, as it totally upgrades the visibility of Tranium's future generations. Note that Anthropic has committed to spending $100B with AWS in the next decade.

Thus, if there’s one big growth vector that I didn’t previously account credibly for, it’s clearly Anthropic.

The AI company has caught up with OpenAI, printing $30 billion in annualized recurring revenue recently.

It has also forced OpenAI to ramp up releasing its model improvements more quickly, as it looks to make up for lost ground in the enterprise space with Claude Code.

Given that Anthropic is arguably short of capacity right now as it deals with the growth inflection spurred by the explosion of Claude Code workloads, this has also benefited Amazon, which most certainly extends to Marvell.

Since we know that because Marvell and Amazon have a well-established relationship with its Trainium generations, we now have line of sight visibility over the next few years, not just in terms of XPUs, but also across the entire networking stack.

Plus, we haven’t even considered the potential of Amazon selling its custom AI chips to third-party customers.

Therefore, I think investors have suddenly awakened to the fact that maybe the 50% growth in the optical portfolio over the past five years might even be surpassed.

Revised estimates from Wall Street indicate that the advanced optical portfolio could double in 2026 and then in 2027 too!

So, all these recent developments arguably will help drive further upside into the forecasts. I believe the market has clearly raised the bar for management to cross when it next reports its earnings on April 28th.

And then, we still haven’t accounted for the co-packaged optics opportunity from 2027, so there’s really a lot going on right now, I think.

It seems like underlying demand is so voracious that companies are desperately looking to find as much compute and networking opportunities as they can right now.

And they are spreading the coverage to as many suppliers and designers as possible.

Marvell & The New Google Surprise Report!

And then, the Google (GOOGL, GOOG) deal!

That one took me by surprise. Because Google has been working with Broadcom for the past seven generations.

We all know that Broadcom charges high margins, right? Broadcom reports overall gross margins of >75%. Yet, it’s also best-in-class, right?

Google’s shifting some work to MediaTek already shows us that the search leader could be keen to find some opportunities to diversify the relationship a bit.

So, I clearly didn’t think that Google could be working with Marvell on two chips, right?

One is a memory processor alongside its famed TPU, while the other is a new custom AI chip specially designed for Google.

I think this clearly validates the technological superiority of Marvell’s stack. This one is likely based on its Structera CXL solutions.

It does seem like the hyperscaler discussions are now coming to fruition for Marvell, and it’s really bringing the “A” game to Broadcom to win over these big tech customers.

For MRVL, it doesn’t need to become the primary designer for Google; it just needs to win some first, and then give Google a second look for future generation needs.

I don’t think Broadcom is going to lower its margins just because of these new imperatives. In contrast, Marvell might see better economics and scale, as it expands into the Google business.

MRVL: Earnings Moving In The Right Direction

Nevertheless, I don’t expect a near-term growth inflection for the current calendar year, but the market looks ahead, right?

Marvell’s forward earnings outlook is already capturing these newer dynamics, and I could see why.

The market is now finally pricing the company as a faster grower than peers.

Not only are the estimates moving in the right direction, but they are also moving up very quickly.

Given the smaller market share as compared to AVGO, the conviction does make sense. Although the question about timing, execution of these projects, was somewhat more questionable back then, before the recent slew of deals.

And, with Amazon expected to ramp up, plus Google now potentially also being part of its customer slate, the roadmap for the next few years looks immensely secure.

Based on the forecasted revenue growth rate through FY2029, we are looking at >30% growth for the current FY, and then another +30% growth for the next FY.

These are rock-solid prognoses, and thus, it didn’t quite make sense that the stock was priced below 25x forward earnings back then.

Yet, Could All These Have Already Been Priced In?

However, even as MRVL appears to have broken out resoundingly against its previous peak, notched in early 2025, the print also looks suspiciously vertical.

If I were to be forthright about the price chart, we mus'nt throw caution to the wind right now.

I would err on the side of caution to see whether we could see a pullback to the January 2025 level first, at the $130 zone.

We can take things from there, and who knows, it might even break down below it, giving us a better opportunity to get in at a lower price point.

Moreover, MRVL is now valued at >43x forward earnings, so well off those lows in March 2026.

And note that MRVL has had trouble getting past the 50x earnings level over the past five years.

Clearly, the market has marked up the optimism so quickly, investors have rushed into the stock too quickly and too fervently, and that’s undisputedly shown in the price action.

I’m not sounding the clarion call for you to cut, because I think MRVL continues to look well-positioned as a buy-and-hold.

Yet, rushing in now when a vertical spike has been printed, and with multiples now flashing caution, doesn’t seem too wise, in my opinion.

Therefore, I think it’s timely for me to step back and reconsider how this breakout will unfold, and assess where the following consolidation zone could be after the optimism cools down.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: IPO Activity Dipped In Q1, But Don't Call It A Downturn
data.description:
By Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist, and Michael Normyle, U.S. Economist and Senior Director at Nasdaq

In the first quarter, there seemed to be one thing after another to create further uncertainty for markets.

  • The Artificial Intelligence (AI)-related selloff that began late last year spilled into early 2026 as investors worried about spending on AI and the businesses it could disrupt.
  • We also saw a(nother) partial government shutdown, impacting the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, which needs to review initial public offering (IPO) filings.
  • Then came the Supreme Court decision to undo the International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs, upending trade policy.

And after all that, the Iran conflict has roiled markets.

Iran conflict boosts energy commodities, but hurts bonds, stocks and gold

From a macro and markets perspective, the biggest impact of the conflict has been on energy, which has rallied significantly from the start of the conflict through the end of March (the relevant period for our IPO Pulses). European natural gas prices were up about 60%, while U.S. and global oil prices were up 40-50% (black bars).

That’s because Iran effectively stopped traffic in the Strait of Hormuz - typically the route for 20% of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies. The conflict has also damaged energy infrastructure across the Persian Gulf region, not just in Iran.

Chart 1: Attacks and Strait of Hormuz closure squeezing commodity supplies, increasing prices

But it wasn’t just energy commodities that saw prices rise. The Persian Gulf region is also a top exporter, often via the Strait of Hormuz, for other key commodities, including:

  • Urea, a fertilizer produced using natural gas, has surged about +35%. The region also makes half the global supply.
  • Aluminum has jumped around +10%, as the region exports a tenth of global supply.
  • Helium, which is an input in semiconductor manufacturing, is not exchange-traded but Qatar exports a third of global supply, so prices have increased.

The higher commodity prices have already boosted the price of gas at the pump, which affects inflation. U.S. headline CPI increased to 3.3% per annum in March, up from 2.4% in February. As a result, U.S. bond yields increased across the yield curve, with the fear that the Federal Reserve will need to raise rates to tackle inflation before it starts a wage-price spiral. U.S. 10-year Treasury yields are up 35 basis points from the start of the conflict through the end of March, meaning bond values fell.

Those higher yields hurt gold prices (-10%), too, making gold relatively less attractive, since it doesn’t pay interest.

While bonds and gold haven’t played their usual role as safe haven assets, the U.S. dollar has (+2%) to a degree. That’s been an added headwind to foreign equities (in dollar terms), which were down 10% or more, while U.S. equity indexes were down just 5% - although this difference also reflects that many foreign companies and economies are much more reliant on energy from the Strait of Hormuz.

Stocks not only sold off but also were more volatile, moving with each new headline, which boosted the VIX by over 25% (red bar).

Still, between the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on April 8 and talks of a peace deal, a lot of these prices have reverted somewhat (circles), including U.S. equities recently rising to record highs.

Iran conflict knocks IPO Pulses from highs to lows… for now

The uncertainty caused by the Iran conflict (and the other disruptions this year) has meant that IPO activity got off to a slow start this year in the U.S. and Stockholm.

Nasdaq U.S. IPO Pulse

Coming into 2026, the first quarter seemed primed for a rebound, as the record 43-day government shutdown in the fourth quarter of last year could have pushed some IPOs into the new year. But that hasn’t been the case for operating companies, with 26 IPOs in the first quarter - a 35% drop from the quarter prior.

Still, overall IPO activity (including special purpose acquisition companies) actually held up, with the first quarter seeing two more IPOs (88) than the fourth quarter (86) - and both quarters raised about $21 billion.

With the Iran conflict driving a selloff in stocks and increasing volatility, it’s weighed on the Nasdaq IPO Pulse, which fell to a nine-month low in March.

But, as we’ve noted before, for the IPO Pulse to truly signal a turn in IPO activity, its change in direction needs to last at least a few months. So far, the IPO Pulse is just two months removed from January’s 15-month high, meaning it’s too soon to suggest the broader uptrend in U.S. IPO activity is over, especially with U.S. equities recovering to record highs and volatility easing in April.

Chart 2: Nasdaq IPO Pulse drops to 9-month low, but too soon to call downturn

Nasdaq Stockholm IPO Pulse

Like in the U.S., Nasdaq Stockholm saw fewer new listings in the first quarter (3) than the fourth quarter of last year (5). Of course, in capital-raised terms, there’s no way to compete with the fourth quarter, when Stockholm had Europe’s biggest IPO in three years - Verisure (VSURE).

And similar to its U.S. counterpart, the Nasdaq Stockholm IPO Pulse fell in March, dropping to a seven-month low. Here, too, since it’s just a one-month drop, it’s premature to call an end to the upturn in Stockholm IPO activity, especially with the IPO Pulse reaching a 20-month high in February. Plus, also like in the U.S., European stocks are nearing record highs in April.

Chart 3: Nasdaq Stockholm IPO Pulse dips to seven-month low from 20-month high

IPO Pulses not yet in downturns, so the first quarter may prove to be a blip

Given the ongoing Iran conflict and other disruptions in the first quarter, it’s no wonder IPO activity got off to a slow start in 2026.

Still, since the dips in the IPO Pulses fall well short of signaling a downturn in IPO activity and U.S. and European equities have rebounded in April, it’s entirely possible that the first quarter’s softer activity will prove to be a blip. We’ll see when we have our next update in July.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon Q1 Preview: Watch The Capex And EBIT Margins Print
data.description:
Performance assessment

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has lagged the broader market index slightly since my last update on the stock.

So I think my Hold view has been appropriate so far.

Elevator pitch

I am getting bullish on Amazon ahead of its earnings release on 29 April '26:

  1. There are 3 key reasons for why I'm not alarmed by high capex spend.
  2. AWS is losing market share but this matters less as absolute growth is strong.
  3. In-house AI chips can improve long term margins.
  4. There is some valuation risk.
  5. AMZN is breaking out and looks ready to make more new highs.

There are 3 key reasons for why I'm not alarmed by high capex spend

One figure many investors will be watching in the upcoming Q1 FY26 release would be capex, especially after the market took the initial news of Amazon's $200B capex spend (vs $150B consensus) negatively.

But I am not as concerned by this high capex spend for a few reasons:

Firstly, the spending is rather broad-based and not merely focused on AI infrastructure:

With such strong demand for our existing offerings and seminal opportunities like AI, chips, robotics, and low earth orbit satellites, we expect to invest about $200 billion in capital expenditures across Amazon in 2026, and anticipate strong long-term return on invested capital.

- CEO Andy Jassy in the Q4 FY25 Press Release.

This makes direct comparisons to Alphabet's (GOOGL, GOOG) $180B capex guidance for FY26 a bit flawed since Alphabet's spend is largely on AI infrastructure in data centers.

Secondly, there is a good amount of visibility and customer lock-in for a bulk of the expected capex spend:

Of the AWS capex we expect to spend in 2026, much of which will be monetized in 2027-2028, we already have customer commitments for a substantial portion of it.

- CEO Andy Jassy in his FY25 shareholder letter.

And thirdly, betting big to seize a dominating position in future markets is not uncharacteristic of Amazon. In fact, it is perfectly in-line with its investment philosophy that Founder Jeff Bezos outlined in 1997, and which is still applicable today (the annual shareholder letters always attach the original 1997 letter to affirm that the same principles still apply each year):

We will continue to make investment decisions in light of long-term market leadership considerations rather than short-term profitability considerations or short-term Wall Street reactions...

We will make bold rather than timid investment decisions where we see a sufficient probability of gaining market leadership advantages. Some of these investments will pay off, others will not, and we will have learned another valuable lesson in either case...

We will balance our focus on growth with emphasis on long-term profitability and capital management. At this stage, we choose to prioritize growth because we believe that scale is central to achieving the potential of our business model.

- Excerpt's from Amazon's investment philosophy outlined in Founder Jeff Bezos' 1997 Letter to Shareholders.

From a numbers perspective, I expect to see about $45B in capex spend in the Q1 release, corresponding to a TTM capex ramp of $149B. I also want to highlight that $200B TTM capex in Q4 FY26 will lead to a more sustained growth ramp if you look at the growth rates trajectory. On the other hand, prior consensus expectations of $150B expected a sharp drop in TTM capex to 17% YoY:

AWS is losing market share, but this matters less as absolute growth is strong

As part of my coverage of all the major cloud vendors (Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet and even Alibaba (BABA)), I track cloud market shares every quarter. And over the past few quarters, I have noticed that Amazon's AWS has been seeing a bit of a decline, losing share to Microsoft's Azure and especially Google Cloud:

However, I think it would be a blunder to place too much weight on this and view this as a headwind because the absolute growth in AWS and its remaining performance obligations [RPOs, which are a leading indicator of future revenues as they represent contracted work yet to be executed and recognized as revenues) is still very strong:

CC refers to constant currency; it takes out the effects of FX. This is useful to assess underlying performance of the business without counting macro factors.

Here are the key drivers of AWS adoption, summarized by the CEO:

First, we have broader capabilities than others, with compelling offerings for model-building... high-performance inference... lower-cost inference (on our custom silicon, Trainium), agent-building... scalable and secure agent environments... turnkey agents for coding, software migrations, and most tasks knowledge workers use...

Second, as customers expand their use of AI, they want their inference to reside near their other applications and data... and much more of it resides in AWS than anywhere else.

Third, as customers expand their AI usage, they consume a lot of additional non-AI services, where AWS also has the broadest and most capable offerings.

And fourth, AWS has the strongest security and operational performance of any AI and infrastructure provider.

- CEO Andy Jassy in his FY25 shareholder letter.

In-house AI chips can improve long term margins

I've recently talked more about hyperscalers investing in their own chips in my recent coverage of Nvidia (NVDA). But for Amazon, I first discussed this strategic move early last year. The big bet Amazon is making is that the market will evolve from prioritizing performance-first to valuing good price to performance metrics. And commentary suggests strong demand and order confirmations to support this:

customers want better price-performance. We’ve seen this movie before. In the CPU space, virtually all of the workloads ran on Intel chips until we invented Graviton in 2018. Graviton, which has up to 40% better price-performance than other x86 processors, is now used expansively by 98% of the top 1,000 EC2 customers...

The same story arc is unfolding in AI. Our second version of our custom AI silicon (Trainium2) had about 30% better price-performance than comparable GPUs, and has largely sold out. Trainium3, which just started shipping at the start of 2026 and is 30-40% more price-performant than Trainium2, is nearly fully-subscribed. A significant chunk of Trainium4, which is still about 18 months from broad availability, has already been reserved.

- CEO Andy Jassy in his FY25 shareholder letter.

EC2 refers to Elastic Compute Cloud, which is Amazon AWS' virtual server service.

According to Jassy, transitioning more to in-house chips can lead to "several hundreds of basis points" improvement in margins. I believe this means we can see mid-high teens EBIT margins in the medium to long term:

But over the short term, I think AI investments and the depreciation costs from that capex may limit some of the margin expansion. Q1 FY26's margin print will give us more clues on the extent of this headwind.

There is some valuation risk

The last time I covered AMZN, it was trading at a 1-yr fwd P/E of <20x. But now, it is priced at a 32.8x. This represents a 50.4% premium to the 21.8x median of its comps:

This 50.4% premium has swung AMZN's relative valuation from the undervalued territory to the overvalued zone, above the typical premium over comps of 27.9%:

Hence, I believe we have to accept that there is some degree of high valuation risk to AMZN stock right now.

AMZN is breaking out and looks ready to make more new highs

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

AMZN Technical Analysis

AMZN stock is printing a big bullish engulf that is breaking monthly resistances, supported by a false breakout or Wyckoff-spring-like initiation. I believe this is a bullish chart setup.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to strong growth in AWS and scope for meaningful margin improvement from in-house AI chips.
  • Valuation: Bearish as AMZN stock is trading more expensively vs peers than usual.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish as the stock shows a compelling breakout setup.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy.
